# Implementation Horizons

This appendix is generated from `book_structure.json`. It is the book-wide build horizon: each chapter has one smallest honest implementation slice and one mature product-level endpoint.

The minimum viable implementation column is not a claim that the implementation already exists. The beyond-state-of-the-art column is a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Support-state movement still requires the source mappings, schemas, proofs, tests, benchmark records, runtime traces, review receipts, or governance artifacts recorded elsewhere in the book.

Current generated coverage: 54 chapter implementation horizons.

## Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance

| Chapter ID | Chapter | Minimum viable implementation | Beyond the state of the art | Support state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `asi-is-a-stack-not-a-model` | ASI Is a Stack, Not a Model | A stack map, layer-boundary table, source crosswalk, and claim ledger that make the architecture navigable before prose is complete. | The beyond-SOTA endpoint is not a larger model, a more elaborate agent loop, or a compound-AI toolkit with a nicer diagram. A mature ASI stack absorbs what scale-first systems, agent loops, compound architectures, cognitive-architecture lineages, and layered-control systems get right while adding the missing contracts: authority ceilings, typed artifacts, evidence states, support-state transitions, replacement transactions, non-claims, rollback paths, and governed recursive-improvement boundaries. | `argument` |
| `the-efficient-asi-hypothesis` | The Efficient ASI Hypothesis | A costed route ledger with quality predicates, residual accounting, and fallback criteria. | The mature endpoint is a governed route economy for cognition, not a larger model, a cheaper model, or a single acceleration trick. Conditional-computation systems, model cascades, compression schemes, fast-generation methods, and broad benchmark suites each attack one cost surface; the Efficient ASI endpoint makes those surfaces comparable through a shared route ledger for task predicates, authority ceilings, selected and rejected candidates, visible cost classes, verification, fallback, residuals, and support-state effects. | `argument` |
| `system-boundaries-and-authority` | System Boundaries and Authority | An authority-transition schema plus a unified authority-tuple lifecycle that binds execution-domain ownership, scope, budget, trace, replay, grant, revocation, cross-domain approval, receipts, and protocol-versus-hardware-root boundaries. | System authority needs a type system for the stack. Every model, tool, memory cell, field, artifact, human, project, and runtime route would carry explicit capability bounds so the architecture can distinguish what a component can do from what it may do. In the mature authority architecture, authority becomes typed, bounded, revocable, and attached to concrete principals, layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and routes. | `argument` |
| `failure-modes-of-ungoverned-intelligence` | Failure Modes of Ungoverned Intelligence | A layered failure taxonomy tied to invariants, source queues, and future tests. | A useful failure model routes ordinary stack breakdowns before they disappear into behavior. It turns vague safety anxiety into typed breakdowns that can be detected, assigned to a layer, blocked, residualized, or escalated before they become invisible stack behavior. At the mature failure-radar boundary, ungoverned intelligence is monitored through ordinary stack breakdowns before those failures can hide behind dramatic or abstract risk language. | `argument` |
| `evidence-states-and-claim-discipline` | Evidence States and Claim Discipline | A claim record, evidence transition record, evidence-cell schema, one valid six-project lineage cell, five expected-invalid mutations, claim/support tables, and validation harnesses that leave the chapter core claim at argument. | Evidence discipline matures into the book's claim-accounting system. It gives every important sentence a governed path from unsupported idea to source-derived, test-backed, mechanized, deprecated, or refuted status without letting polish, enthusiasm, or release hygiene stand in for truth. In the mature evidence ledger, every major claim carries both a claim label and a support state, and movement requires source ingestion, prototype inspection, proof/spec evidence, benchmark evidence, or actual tests appropriate to the transition. | `argument` |
| `scalable-oversight-and-adversarial-ai-control` | Scalable Oversight and Adversarial AI Control | A versioned oversight-protocol record with task/risk/authority scope, supervisor and system envelopes, information-access declaration, role and incentive configuration, direct-review baseline, independent outcome-audit status, correlation and persuasion residuals, stop condition, escalation owner, and bounded downstream handoff. Eight finite Lean theorems and a seven-case deterministic fixture bridge route missing evidence views, dependency disclosure, baseline, high-risk audit, abstention evidence, and authority separation without claiming oversight efficacy. | A mature oversight control plane selects direct review, assisted consultation, structured adversarial review, or abstention based on task risk, access asymmetry, supervisor capability, calibrated outcome evidence, correlation risk, and available accountable escalation. It continuously tests protocol performance against held-out outcomes and stronger baselines while preserving dissent, auditability, operator-load residuals, and the separation between supervision, training, and execution authority. This is a target architecture, not a claim that the repository has solved weak-to-strong oversight, debate, alignment, safety, or autonomous control. | `argument` |
| `human-intent-as-a-formal-input` | Human Intent as a Formal Input | An intent-contract schema and example transformations from request to governed task. | Human intent enters the mature stack through a compiler that turns goals into bounded command contracts. It preserves goals, constraints, authority, evidence requirements, artifacts, stop conditions, ambiguity, and consent so downstream capability cannot treat a loose request as unlimited permission. The mature intent compiler admits intent as a structured agreement, not a prompt-shaped wish: goals, constraints, authority, artifacts, evidence requirements, ambiguities, and stop conditions travel together. | `argument` |
| `constitutional-alignment-substrate` | Constitutional Alignment: Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility | A compact constitution plus an agency-rights checklist, with fixtures for protected predicates, conflict routing, review routes, rights usability, rollback, appeal, and self-modification weakening. | A mature constitutional layer compiles protected commitments into plan, tool, memory, routing, human-control, and self-improvement gates while keeping correction, refusal, appeal, rollback, explanation, exit, audit, and accountable delegation materially available. It remains a constraint compiler and review surface, not proof that moral correctness, dignity preservation, consent quality, reviewer independence, or deployed safety has been solved. | `argument` |
| `moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict` | Moral Uncertainty, Value Conflict, and Contestable Governance | A value-conflict record plus governance-right receipt suite validates residual uncertainty, dissent, bounded decisions, audit material, redaction appeal paths, exit and fork access, and safety obligations without claiming moral correctness or deployed governance. | A mature contestability layer carries unresolved value conflicts into durable rights receipts, appeal surfaces, audit artifacts, safety-limited forks, portable exit paths, replacement-preserved dissent, and self-improvement gates. It lets downstream planning, memory, runtime, SCF replacement, evidence, and recursive self-improvement see which authority was narrowed, which residual obligations survived, what was withheld, and how affected parties can challenge or leave without claiming moral finality, legal adequacy, institutional legitimacy, export usability, fork safety, or deployed enforcement. | `argument` |
| `stable-capability-fields` | Stable Capability Fields | An SCF record schema with field identity, implementation versions, lifecycle state, qualification context, evidence, route validity, migration path, and rollback metadata. | Stable capability fields give the stack a capability-identity and replacement market. It lets implementations improve, migrate, split, merge, retire, or roll back while the consuming system continues to know which capability is qualified, under what authority, with which evidence and residuals. A mature capability field preserves identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, regression memory, and rollback policy while implementations change. | `argument` |
| `capability-replacement-and-rollback` | Capability Replacement and Rollback | A replacement transaction schema with precheck, gate, commit, monitor, and rollback phases. | Capability replacement only works as a transaction control plane for the whole stack. Every candidate implementation enters as a transaction, not a hope: prior artifact, candidate artifact, field identity, authority ceiling, regression floor, evaluator boundary, canary scope, monitor window, rollback receipt, and residual inheritance are all first-class objects. In that end state, a capability can improve continuously without making the system forget what it used to know. Regressions follow the field forward, not the old implementation backward. Failed canaries become residuals. Rollbacks are rehearsed and owned. State migration is classified as reversible, compensatable, forked, or residual rather than treated as an implementation detail. A default route is therefore never just the latest winner; it is the candidate that survived the transaction while preserving the floor. | `argument` |
| `security-kernel-and-digital-scifs` | Security Kernel and Digital SCIFs | A secure-handle workflow and SCIF lifecycle diagram. | The security kernel's end state is a least-exposure operating layer for high-agency AI. The model can reason about a task, request authority, and receive sanitized results, but secrets, protected memory, credentials, private sources, and privileged actions live behind leases, handles, clearance checks, compartment lifecycles, and audit receipts. In that end state, sensitive work has a visible lifecycle: request, denial or scoped approval, context admission, substitution, execution, sanitization, zeroization, commit, residual leak-risk classification, expiry, and revocation. The product surface does not ask users or researchers to trust that the model was careful. It shows which authority was used, which material was withheld, which output was sanitized, which logs are available, and which residual risks remain. | `argument` |
| `model-weight-custody-and-hardware-roots-of-trust` | Model-Weight Custody and Hardware Roots of Trust | A versioned weight-custody record with model/artifact identity, lineage, encrypted-storage reference, custody authority, key-release policy, attestation requirement/result, environment identity, access scope, revocation path, residual owner, and requested load. A finite Lean route blocks a requested load with required but invalid attestation. | A mature custody control plane continuously inventories model and derivative artifacts, binds encrypted storage and transfer to named identities, releases keys only to independently verified current trust domains, captures measurement/policy lineage, tracks copies and recipient scopes, supports scoped revocation and incident response, and treats open-weight distribution as a separately governed irreversible release. It integrates hardware/firmware trust assumptions, vendor and operator boundaries, runtime authority, monitoring, and residual risk without claiming any attestation proves the model safe or the system trustworthy. This is a target architecture, not a claim that the repository has a custody system, confidential environment, effective weight security, or authorized deployment. | `argument` |
| `ai-supply-chain-integrity-and-lifecycle-provenance` | AI Supply-Chain Integrity and Lifecycle Provenance | A versioned supply-chain admission record with artifact identity and digest, lineage reference, supplier/distributor scope, build or training provenance reference, signature requirement/result, component inventory reference/freshness, advisory state, revocation path, residual owner, and requested artifact admission. A finite Lean route quarantines a requested artifact with an unresolved critical advisory and blocks a requested artifact with a required but unverified signature. | A mature AI supply-chain control plane maintains a continuously updated graph across data acquisition, curation, code and dependency changes, build and training runs, model and derivative artifacts, signing and verification, suppliers and distributors, deployment packages, vulnerability and advisory feeds, incident response, revocation, retention, and disposal. It can identify affected downstream paths, preserve decision history, request bounded re-review, and exchange interoperable BOM and provenance references without conflating transparency with security or safety. This is a target architecture, not a claim that this repository has complete lineage, a secure training or build pipeline, trustworthy suppliers, verified signatures, effective revocation, compliant distribution, safe models, or authorized deployment. | `argument` |
| `recursive-self-improvement-boundaries` | Recursive Self-Improvement Boundaries | A self-improvement protocol that can reject, quarantine, roll back, or retire a proposed change. | Recursive improvement belongs inside a governed improvement market, not a runaway loop. Components may propose patches, tools, routes, policy updates, benchmarks, teacher edits, and architecture changes, but promotion flows through stable fields, replacement transactions, independent evaluators, protected-invariant checks, boundary-delta review, monitor windows, and rollback ownership. In that end state, the system can improve faster because the rules are sharper. Cheap interventions are tried before expensive or irreversible ones. Verification, security, human review, and rollback budgets are protected from being "optimized away." Readiness gates expire when the frontier, architecture, source corpus, lifecycle state, or threat model changes. A candidate may generate evidence, but it cannot be the only judge of whether the evidence is enough. | `argument` |
| `open-ended-improvement-engines` | Open-Ended Improvement Engines | Implemented as seven digest-bound improvement-campaign records and ten rejecting mutations covering complete review handoff, independent qualification, budget, stop authority, failure-history retention, residual ownership, and authority laundering. It is a finite routing bridge, not a campaign engine. | The mature system runs multiple constrained discovery campaigns across simulations, program spaces, data curation, and tools while preserving evaluator separation, challenge provenance, counterexamples, resource economics, sandbox limits, dual-use review, reproducibility, and campaign stop authority. It uses generated discoveries only through qualified capability fields and governed replacement, rather than granting a search process the power to promote itself. This is a target architecture, not a claim of autonomous scientific discovery, safe recursive self-improvement, or open-ended general intelligence. | `argument` |

## Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution

| Chapter ID | Chapter | Minimum viable implementation | Beyond the state of the art | Support state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `intent-to-execution-contracts` | Command Contracts: From Intent to Executable Work | Intent-contract, command-contract, intent-execution-trace, and intent-origin fixtures plus a synthetic plan-execution harness with valid and expected-invalid authority, receipt, mismatch, requirement-loss, cycle, ambiguity, hidden-override, authority-widening, field-confidence, and inferred-authority cases. | A mature command-contract spine gives humans, planners, verifiers, job runners, policy layers, runtime adapters, and later AI agents one semantic control language for executable work. Every serious request would enter as a durable intake object, receive an explicit authority envelope, lower through command, plan, job, artifact, verification, delivery, feedback, and residual records, and remain inspectable after the work is done. Every field would carry provenance and confidence; every side-effect route would have an authority basis; every plan node would trace back to the contract it serves; every job would have a dispatch receipt; every artifact would have an identity, verifier, delivery record, feedback state, and residual ledger. This endpoint remains speculative architecture until parser tests, dispatch enforcement, approval-path checks, tool-effect controls, replayed vertical traces, negative controls, and accepted evidence-transition records exist. | `argument` |
| `planning-as-a-control-layer` | Planning as a Control Layer: DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage | A public plan graph plus PlanForge DAG record surface with fixtures and validation for lifecycle states, dependency order, dispatch receipts, authority, capability tiers, adequacy contracts, cost-quality ledgers, residuals, invalid cycles, contract mismatch, requirement loss, unreceipted dispatch, approval bypass, scheduler-state boundaries, and runtime-replan delta shape, while leaving decomposition quality, selected-tier adequacy, route quality, live feedback handling, runtime replanning, and deployed scheduler behavior unclaimed. | The mature version is a governed scheduler and capacity allocator for cognitive work. It receives accepted command contracts, builds and maintains plan graphs, and decides which nodes are proposed, blocked, reviewable, dispatchable, dispatched, replanned, residualized, or stopped. It never owns memory, authority, verification, or side effects; it coordinates the handoffs that let those layers do their own jobs. Each node carries dependency order, context demand, authority state, capability tier, adequacy contract, verifier, cost envelope, route assignment, merge condition, escalation path, and residual behavior. Intelligence arbitrage is measured only after verification, repair, merge, human review, and residual cost are counted, so cheaper work is accepted only when it satisfies the node adequacy contract and remains useful to downstream dependents. | `argument` |
| `cognitive-compilation-and-semantic-ir` | Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR | A semantic IR sketch and compile/verify/repair loop for one artifact type. | The mature version is a compiler toolchain for cognitive work. It turns goals, command contracts, plans, and source context into semantic intermediate representations before producing code, prose, schemas, proof targets, experiments, or tool jobs. The central product is not the generated artifact; it is the obligation-preserving build graph that explains why the artifact should exist and what it must continue to satisfy. In a cognitive compiler, every important artifact has a lowering receipt. The receipt names the source plan, semantic atoms, obligations, assumptions, validators, target constraints, authority requirements, repair scope, support-state effect, and residuals that produced it. A later writer, model, compiler pass, or reviewer can change one node without erasing unrelated obligations or pretending that a syntactic pass is semantic acceptance. | `argument` |
| `virtual-context-abi` | The Virtual Context ABI: Typed Pages, Cells, and Certificates | A context ABI record plus semantic-page certificate fixtures validated by the context admission/adequacy harness, covering address, version, mount, snapshot, representation, source refs, authority ceiling, loss/use contract, adequacy state, residuals, and fault behavior. | A mature Virtual Context ABI is the memory syscall layer for long-horizon AI work: planners, verifiers, workers, claim ledgers, reader editions, and auditors request typed context objects by stable address, version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority label, adequacy target, certificate, lease, and residual boundary. The endpoint is a governed context fabric where exact source excerpts, summaries, redactions, planning briefs, claim cells, contradiction pairs, reader paraphrases, and execution packets can derive from shared sources without pretending they have the same authority or evidential role. | `argument` |
| `context-transactions-snapshots-mounts-and-taint` | Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint | A transaction model for context reads, writes, branches, and deletion closure. | The mature version is a transactional memory substrate for AI context. It does not treat memory as a vector store with annotations. It treats every context view as the product of committed events, versioned pages, mount policies, snapshots, branch rules, taint propagation, deletion obligations, declassification decisions, and replay boundaries. Under transactional context memory, every materialized context view can explain itself. It can show which immutable events were visible, which branch was used, which mounts were readable or writable, which cells were actually read, which derivatives were created, which taint labels propagated, which deletion obligations closed, which contradictions were recorded, and why a view was allowed to feed a job, artifact, claim, or release. | `argument` |
| `verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy` | Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy | A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states. | The mature version is a verification-capacity control plane. It does not reward a system for loading more text. It asks whether the loaded semantic units, representations, tools, reviewers, and comparison mode are strong enough for the exact claim, risk level, and support-state effect being requested. When verification capacity is governed, every important claim has an adequacy record before it receives a stronger support label. The record names the source units, compression path, verification mode, negative evidence, residual risks, and escalation route. A context packet adequate for orientation can remain drafting-only; a packet adequate for one local citation can still be inadequate for a cross-document safety claim; a proof check over a finite predicate can stay separate from an empirical deployment claim. | `argument` |
| `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` | Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision | A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example. | The mature version is an epistemic version-control system. It does not merely annotate chapters with confidence labels. It gives every material claim a durable identity, provenance, support state, uncertainty record, contradiction state, surface references, revision history, promotion blockers, and escalation path. With a claim ledger in force, prose is a view over the ledger rather than the source of belief. A claim can appear in a chapter, diagram, appendix, release note, reader edition, benchmark report, or runtime artifact without losing its identity. If new evidence weakens it, every surface that depends on the claim can be found. If a contradiction appears, the ledger can downgrade, split, merge, deprecate, residualize, or route the claim to Spinoza, UAT, or human review. | `argument` |
| `spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims` | Proof-Carrying Claims and Adversarial Review | A proof-carrying-claim schema and tribunal-review schema with synthetic valid and expected-invalid fixtures for narrow formal passes, citation no-change, mismatch escalation, missing artifacts, failed verification, high-risk review, dissent, and prior-review laundering. | The product-level endpoint is a verification operating system that routes each high-value claim or high-risk artifact to the cheapest adequate verifier or review process, records interpretation mappings, failed attempts, timeouts, dissent, required actions, and non-claims, then writes bounded effects back to claim ledgers and execution constraints without confusing artifact validity, verdict scope, semantic adequacy, or support-state movement. | `argument` |
| `labor-os-and-typed-jobs` | Labor OS and Typed Jobs | A typed-job schema with lifecycle states and permission checks. | The mature version is an operating system for bounded cognitive labor. It does not run "agents" as ambient actors. It admits work only as typed jobs whose parent contract, plan node, lifecycle state, permission basis, approval record, workspace, adapter route, expected artifact, failure behavior, audit trail, delivery state, and evidence state are explicit. In a labor OS, every job can be refused, approved, dispatched, adjudicated, delivered, quarantined, replayed, or retired through declared transitions. Delivery and evidence readiness remain separate: a user-visible output may exist while verification, artifact capture, replay declaration, or residual accounting is still incomplete. That separation prevents useful output from being laundered into governed evidence. | `argument` |
| `artifact-graphs-audit-logs-and-replay` | Artifact Graphs, Audit Logs, and Replay | An artifact graph schema with job, source, context, and evidence edges plus a projection/revocation record that checks canonical-versus-projection state, reverse-dependency closure, replay grade, acknowledged terminal completion, and relocation-safe identity. | The mature version is an evidence graph for work products. It does not merely store files. It records the lineage, role, provenance, replay grade, environment assumptions, context transactions, semantic certificates, audit events, claim links, test links, residuals, and evidence gate for every artifact that the stack may later reuse. Inside an evidence graph, artifacts can become context, evidence, regression cases, release inputs, compression targets, procedural-memory traces, or reader-edition material only through declared gates. A path on disk is not enough. The artifact node must say what created it, what sources and context shaped it, what can be replayed, what cannot be replayed, and which claims or tests it is allowed to affect. | `argument` |
| `runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval` | Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval | A runtime-adapter contract with permissions, approval requirements, result evidence, finite route predicates, a synthetic permission harness, a public-safe local effect replay, and an adversarial boundary probe for parentage, authority, approval, sandbox, secret, rollback, receipt, audit, support-state, and non-claim controls. | The mature version is a capability firewall for external effects. It does not expose tools as ambient functions. It grants scoped, expiring effect leases to typed jobs after checking target, capability, sandbox, authority handle, approval requirement, risk tier, pre-state, post-state expectations, rollback path, and evidence obligations. With capability adapters, a successful tool response is not the end of the story. The adapter returns an effect receipt that separates request, approval, execution, verification, rollback, irreversible residuals, incident refs, audit refs, and support-state effect. Approval is also scoped: approving one deployment, file edit, message, payment, hardware action, or credential use does not authorize every similar future action. | `argument` |
| `inter-stack-protocols-identity-and-economic-exchange` | Inter-Stack Protocols, Identity, and Economic Exchange | Nine digest-bound synthetic exchange records and eleven rejecting mutations bridge owned Lean routes for complete local handoff, missing sender, audience mismatch, expiry, invalid or revoked credentials, missing reserved budget, disputed receipt, and missing residual ownership. No peer, protocol, credential, payment, account, or remote effect ran. | A mature stack exposes a protocol-neutral exchange control plane that maps tool requests, agent tasks, federated service calls, identity presentations, and value-bearing requests into bounded local contracts. It tracks discovery separately from trust, credentials separately from authorization, reservations separately from settlement, and declared results separately from independently reviewed evidence; it propagates expiry, revocation, endpoint change, budget release, dispute, and residual ownership to affected requests before existing local owners permit the next effect. This is a target architecture, not a claim of a local protocol, identity system, credential framework, payment network, peer-trust model, authorization service, task verifier, settlement system, privacy program, safe distributed deployment, or ASI. | `argument` |
| `procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure` | Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure | A loop closure pipeline from trace detection to verified tool record. | The mature version is a procedure foundry for verified reusable cognition. It does not merely cache prompts or scripts. It mines artifact traces, clusters comparable work, preserves failures and near misses, abstracts the invariant structure, discovers parameters, synthesizes candidate procedures, verifies regressions, attaches monitoring and retirement rules, and then offers qualified tools to Part III routing. In procedural memory, reusable cognition has states rather than vibes: candidate trace, loop cluster, tool candidate, verified reuse, routable tool, quarantined tool, retired tool. A trace cannot jump straight into routable behavior because it looks elegant. The procedure card must carry source traces, rejected traces, near misses, abstraction rationale, preconditions, postconditions, SCF target, benchmark floor, authority requirement, monitoring signal, retirement trigger, and non-claims. | `argument` |

## Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates

| Chapter ID | Chapter | Minimum viable implementation | Beyond the state of the art | Support state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `routing-heads-and-specialist-cores` | Routing Heads and Specialist Cores | A router registry with capability metadata, cost, authority, and fallback rules. | Routing becomes strategic when it is a governed capability market inside the stack. It is not a bigger tool picker. It is a control boundary that can select among specialists, proof checkers, retrieval lanes, local runtime cores, human review, and fallback routes while preserving the authority, evidence, and residual obligations attached to the choice. In the mature routing market, route selection is a governed lease, not a suggestion engine: a lightweight head selects bounded specialists only with the authority, context, tools, fallback, and residual ownership the task permits. | `argument` |
| `readiness-gates-residual-escrow-and-quarantine` | Readiness Gates, Residual Escrow, and Quarantine | A module lifecycle state machine and residual escrow ledger. | Readiness is the lifecycle control plane for every routable or replaceable capability in the stack. It is the mechanism that lets the architecture improve without confusing novelty, benchmark wins, or source-reported success with permission for ordinary use. In the mature readiness control plane, readiness is a lifecycle state with evidence, residuals, expiry, allowed routes, blocked routes, rollback consequences, and review ownership. | `argument` |
| `personal-compute-hives-and-federated-edge-intelligence` | Personal Compute Hives and Federated Edge Intelligence | A local personal-hive dashboard with a device registry, portal cards, signed resource cards, job queue, manual phone approval receipts, policy-first scheduler, job bids, federation leases disabled by default, artifact log, and no public federation or autonomous spending. | Personal compute hives become valuable as an owned compute fabric for personal, family, project, and rented capacity. It lets the ASI Stack place work near the right data and device without turning reachability into authority, convenience into surveillance, or cheap compute into a privacy leak. In the mature personal-hive fabric, owned and rented devices form a policy-bounded compute fabric only when scheduling respects capability, locality, trust, cost, energy, data class, physical risk, and authority. | `argument` |
| `compact-generative-systems-and-residual-honesty` | Compact Generative Systems: Generate, Verify, Repair, and Residual Honesty | A compact generative record plus compression receipt fixture suite with one residual-burden case, one lossy or blocked exactness case, one failed-verification case, one repair-or-fallback case, and one consumer-policy rejection. The folded semantic-representation slice adds `semantic_node_record.valid.json` as a record-shape fixture for provenance, hierarchy, relation refs, tokenization contract, grounding state, versioning, supersession, residual uncertainty, permitted uses, evaluation refs, and consumer-policy limits; it remains record-shape validation only. | A mature compression control plane treats compact generators, generated reconstructions, exact repairs, scoped residuals, consumer policies, fallback, replay, and resource accounting as one audited representation market. It lets the stack choose the cheapest lawful representation for a task while preserving verifier quality, repair burden, exactness boundaries, declared loss, fallback availability, and non-claims. This remains a target architecture until public codec, verifier, utility, rate, and replay evidence exists. The mature compression layer also leases semantic representations. Graph nodes, tree paths, and semantic tokens become task-scoped meaning objects whose provenance, grounding, adequacy, interoperability, supersession, consumer policy, and residual uncertainty travel with every use. They may reduce burden only when they preserve the constraints required by the consumer and fall back to fuller context, source artifacts, or quarantine when they do not. | `argument` |
| `fast-generation-architectures` | Fast Generation Architectures | A generation-mode registry and benchmark record with task risk, latency budget, context packet, mode, draft source, verifier, acceptance predicate, accepted tokens, wall-clock time, quality or pass result, compute and memory notes, fallback, and promotion decision. | The endpoint for fast generation is a governed generation-mode control plane. It lets the stack choose among slow, fast, speculative, parallel, recurrent, cached, and hybrid generation routes without allowing raw throughput to masquerade as capability or evidence. In the mature generation-mode controller, faster generation becomes routable only when task risk, accepted-output quality, verifier cost, fallback rate, latency, compute, memory, and serving pressure are measured together. | `argument` |
| `governed-deliberation-and-test-time-scaling` | Governed Deliberation and Test-Time Scaling | Ten digest-bound synthetic deliberation records and eleven rejecting mutations bridge ten owned Lean routes for complete planning handoff, budget/mode/verifier/history/stop/residual completeness, trace-authority separation, exhaustion, and high-risk independent verification. Fifteen known extra-compute harms remain regression-only knowledge. | A mature deliberation control plane chooses direct generation, bounded revision, candidate search, or abstention by risk, expected verification value, budget, and independent-check availability. It measures quality, verifier cost, branch count, latency, residuals, and counterexamples without turning a trace or benchmark score into authority. This is a target architecture, not a claim of local reasoning quality, reliable test-time scaling, safe self-reflection, or autonomous general intelligence. | `argument` |
| `rankfold-neuralfold-and-artifact-compression` | RankFold, NeuralFold, and Artifact Compression | A compressed artifact record with ratio, residual, probe, fallback, and utility fields, plus a public-safe local RankFold replay probe and artifact-import record for synthetic roundtrip behavior and existing archive metadata/verifier summaries. | Artifact compression needs an admission system before compressed forms can enter the stack. It treats compressed forms as routed candidates that must earn each use case instead of inheriting the authority of the full artifact. In the mature artifact-admission lane, compressed artifacts are candidates for specific uses, not replacements for the original, until probes, decode behavior, residual coding, fallback, and consumer policy accept them. | `argument` |
| `resource-economics-and-token-budgets` | Resource Economics and Token Budgets | A resource ledger with budget, risk, cost, quality, verification tax, protected-overhead, displaced-cost, and evidence-reference fields, plus a companion Simulation Contract Record for synthetic or simulated results with scope, fidelity, temporal semantics, resource bill, bottlenecks, omissions, instrumentation effects, transfer decision, support-state effect, residual risks, and non-claims. | Resource economics matures into a risk-aware budget operating system and claim-transport layer for cognition. It decides when the stack may spend, defer, shrink, escalate, reject, residualize work, or downgrade synthetic evidence without letting local cost pressure delete verification, security, approval, replay, rollback, human review, fidelity limits, or transfer boundaries. In the mature budget OS, cognition has a budget and simulations have contracts: budgets cannot delete verification tax, protected overhead, review capacity, rollback cost, security cost, residual burden, or the requirement that simulated results travel only as far as declared scope, fidelity, resources, omissions, and transfer decisions allow. | `argument` |
| `mathematical-and-search-substrates` | Mathematical and Search Substrates | A technical-substrate appendix plan with experiment matrix, adoption gates, and a deterministic substrate-adoption trace that preserves baselines, negative controls, consumer-axis limits, fallback, retirement/refutation paths, residuals, and non-claim boundaries without claiming substrate usefulness. | Mathematical and search substrates need an adoption exchange before they can affect ordinary routes. It keeps unusual mathematics available for narrow specialist routes while making every adoption decision baseline-bound, falsifiable, consumer-gated, and reversible. In the mature substrate adoption exchange, mathematical substrates remain optional specialists until baseline-symmetric workloads show where they improve search, routing, compression, representation, and verifier workload without hiding tradeoffs. | `argument` |
| `circle-calculus-and-proof-carrying-ai-contracts` | Circle Calculus and Proof-Carrying AI Contracts | A contract record with theorem IDs, proof status, content fingerprint, deterministic fields, validation commands, consumer check, ordinary baselines, and non-claims. | In its finished form, Circle proof-carrying contracts act as a proof-contract transport service. It moves finite structural facts through the ASI Stack with theorem references, deterministic fields, fingerprints, resolver state, replay commands, consumer gates, and non-claims intact. In the mature proof-contract transport service, proof-carrying AI contracts transport finite structural facts as theorem-linked receipts, while consumer gates preserve validation commands, fingerprints, resolver state, and explicit non-claims. | `argument` |
| `coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts` | Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts | A contract-backed memory and attention evaluation plan with slot, winding, coverage, freshness, active-token, loop-exit, work-budget, and baseline fields. | Coil memory is useful only when it becomes a memory-admission and recurrence-control plane. It makes slot, lag, freshness, aliasing, active-work, and loop-exit facts available to the stack without confusing those facts with retrieval quality or reasoning success. In the mature recurrence-control plane, coil memory provides structural guardrails for slots, lag, freshness, coverage, recurrence, and work budgets while leaving retrieval quality and reasoning quality to separate evidence. | `argument` |
| `coilra-multicoil-rope-and-cyclic-mixers` | CoilRA, MultiCoil RoPE, and Cyclic Mixers | A mixer and position-substrate experiment matrix with structural proof references, ordinary baselines, quality/runtime/memory/parameter metrics, alias and load diagnostics, and explicit non-claims. | The endpoint for cyclic mixers is an evaluation lane, not an assumed upgrade path. It lets the stack test phase, recurrence, residue/winding, relative-position, circulant, and block-cyclic ideas without spending structural proof as if it were quality, runtime, memory, or deployment evidence. In the mature cyclic-substrate evaluation lane, cyclic mixers are optional specialist substrates until structural receipts, diagnostics, hardware constraints, and baseline-symmetric tradeoff packets justify a scoped canary route. | `argument` |

## Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book

| Chapter ID | Chapter | Minimum viable implementation | Beyond the state of the art | Support state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `executable-specifications-and-lean-proof-envelope` | Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope | A proof manifest, proof target schema and fixture, Lean workspace, proof-readiness validator, and proof artifact traceability audit report for all implemented finite-record targets. | The formal-methods endpoint is a claims control plane for the whole stack. It does not try to prove "ASI safety" or "alignment" as one grand theorem. It maintains a live registry of every formal-looking promise, routes each promise to the right artifact lane, and refuses to let a passed artifact support a broader claim than it actually checks. A mature proof envelope makes every chapter, protocol, release, and runtime module consume proof only through a typed Proof Target Record. The record would name the exact predicate, source claim, artifact lane, verifier command, verifier result, semantic adequacy review, consumer requirements, support-state effect, limitations, and non-claims. A Lean predicate, JSON Schema, process validator, behavior test, benchmark, external theorem reference, and research backlog item would remain different objects with different authority. | `argument` |
| `benchmark-ratchets-and-anti-goodhart-evidence` | Benchmark Ratchets and Anti-Goodhart Evidence | A benchmark ratchet record plus a metric-provenance record that preserves origin class, closure inheritance, target capacity, retry lineage, checkpoint/output binding, blockers, and non-claims, with one blocked lineage fixture and rejecting mutations. | The benchmark endpoint is an operating system for evaluation, not a leaderboard appendix. Every evaluation would become a governed evidence packet with enough history to tell whether the system learned a capability, learned a test, leaked a public calibration surface, or merely improved a proxy. A mature benchmark OS manages benchmarks as instruments with lifecycle state. A task can begin as a candidate metric, become a frontier probe, graduate into a regression floor, enter contamination review, be retired, or remain blocked. Success would never move a claim by itself; it would open a promotion review that compares baselines, residuals, negative results, holdouts, transfer checks, mutation checks, public-calibration limits, and the exact claim being considered. | `argument` |
| `capability-thresholds-and-deployment-commitments` | Capability Thresholds and Deployment Commitments | Eight digest-bound synthetic threshold-commitment records and five rejecting mutations bridge eight owned Lean routes for crossed and non-crossed readiness handoffs, missing evaluation scope, baseline, uncertainty, residual custody, safeguard record, and safeguard verification. No capability evaluation or release ran. | A mature capability-commitment control plane maintains a versioned portfolio of domain-specific thresholds with public or reviewable evaluation envelopes, uncertainty and coverage records, precommitted safeguards, independent verification, residual-risk review, prospective exception governance, and automatic re-evaluation when models, scaffolds, elicitation, or threat models change. It coordinates release, security, monitoring, access, rollback, and disclosure obligations without reducing diverse dangerous-capability profiles to one autonomy scalar. This is a target architecture, not a claim that this repository has measured capability, set an adequate threshold, verified safeguards, or achieved safe deployment. | `argument` |
| `adversarial-evaluation-sandbagging-and-training-time-deception` | Adversarial Evaluation, Sandbagging, and Training-Time Deception | Eight digest-bound synthetic evaluation-integrity records and five rejecting mutations bridge eight owned Lean routes for complete review handoff, selection/reward/monitor provenance, independent evaluation, cross-context probes, discrepancy quarantine, and intent-laundering rejection. No model or detector ran. | The mature system continuously tests how its own evidence depends on observation through diversified evaluators, context perturbations, trusted and untrusted monitor separation, blinded holdouts where feasible, independent replay, countercases, and assurance-state handoff into training leases and deployment gates. This is a target architecture, not a claim of complete deception detection, universal evaluator trust, or safety. | `argument` |
| `safety-cases-and-structured-assurance` | Safety Cases and Structured Assurance | A versioned assurance-case record with one scoped top claim, deployment and hazard context, argument/evidence references, assumptions, defeaters, countercase-review state, acceptance criterion, residual owner, decision authority, and affected release path. A finite Lean route sends a requested release with an unresolved defeater to accountable review rather than readiness release. | A mature assurance compiler continuously builds auditable, versioned cases from the book's evidence, evaluation, threshold, safeguard, readiness, provenance, and residual records; it highlights stale dependencies, missing countercase searches, evidence-scope mismatch, contradiction, and changed deployment conditions; requests independent review; and feeds only a bounded case status to existing release and authority gates. It preserves dissent and failure history, supports multiple threat-model arguments without combining them into a single confidence score, and makes a case revision a reviewable deployment event. This is a target architecture, not a claim that this repository has a complete case, valid threat model, adequate evidence, effective controls, safe system, or authorized deployment. | `argument` |
| `policy-optimization-and-learning-from-feedback` | Policy Optimization and Learning from Feedback | A policy optimization record schema, fixture, and finite Lean predicates that separate feedback, update constraints, evaluation, governance gates, rollback, and non-claims from unrun training results. | The learning endpoint is a governed actuator for behavior change across the entire stack. Policy optimization would not be a fine-tuning side quest attached to the generator. It would be the controlled mechanism by which planners, routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution policies, generation modes, and governance assistants become better without gaining new authority by training side effect. A mature policy-update lane treats each update as a behavior-change lease. The lease would name the target policy, old and new behavior, admissible feedback, reward or preference boundary, update family, drift bound, holdouts, regressions, reward-hacking probes, authority effect, rollback plan, monitor window, deployment scope, evidence packets, residuals, and non-claims. PPO-like, DPO-like, GRPO-like, RLVR-like, context-policy, router-policy, or custom control-policy updates would be implementation choices under this record, not substitutes for governance. | `argument` |
| `data-engines-continual-learning-and-unlearning` | Data Engines, Continual Learning, and Unlearning | A viable first slice is a versioned data-admission receipt, a public-safe synthetic fixture, deterministic routing for missing controls, and a lineage ledger that keeps every candidate experimental until an independently evaluated training or deletion path is accepted. | The mature target is a stack-wide data governance plane that treats every learning input, synthetic descendant, replay item, deletion request, evaluation split, and derived model artifact as an auditable lifecycle object. It measures workload-specific coverage, contamination, utility, cost, privacy, freshness, and deletion propagation; compares replacement, accumulation, replay, quarantine, and retraining policies; and requires a verifier-backed lineage closure before a result can affect deployment or belief. This is a target architecture, not a current system result: support remains argument until scoped intake, training, unlearning, and descendant-verification evidence is independently replayed and accepted. | `argument` |
| `artifact-steward-agents-and-living-project-governance` | Artifact Steward Agents and Living Project Governance | A `PROJECT_STEWARD.yml` file plus a GitHub-oriented steward bot that labels issues, drafts plans, proposes work contracts, requests CI, summarizes pull requests, writes release notes, records evidence, opens sunset reviews, and never spends funds or merges to protected branches without policy approval. | A durable-artifact stack ultimately needs a project-lifecycle operating system. The steward would not be a project owner, treasurer, maintainer monopoly, or autonomous manager with implied rights. It would be a bounded continuity service that keeps mission, memory, roadmap, work, funds, compute, contributors, evidence, releases, governance, and sunset criteria inspectable across many human and AI work cycles. A mature steward OS would let every durable artifact carry an Artifact Steward Charter plus typed records for work contracts, contribution ledgers, treasury policy, event taint, steward action decisions, release evidence, federation contracts, and sunset review. The steward would propose, prepare, coordinate, and execute only inside explicit authority. Humans, maintainers, communities, project hives, personal hives, CI runners, and rented compute would receive scoped contracts rather than inheriting project authority. | `argument` |
| `integrated-reference-architecture` | Integrated Reference Architecture | A reference flow diagram, interface table, deterministic reference-trace fixtures, and a seven-stage name-to-effect trace that binds request-derived input, policy, canonical state, observed effect, acknowledged receipt, evaluator, and claim boundary while preserving blockers and non-claims. | The integration endpoint is a trace kernel for the whole ASI Stack. The stack would not be a diagram of cooperating layers; it would be an inspectable machine that can explain what authority entered, which artifacts were produced, what evidence changed, which residuals survived, and why a path was promoted, blocked, narrowed, rolled back, or escalated. A mature reference trace kernel would make every significant run emit a Reference Trace Record. The record would connect human intent, constitutional authority, command contracts, plan DAGs, VCM packets, route decisions, claim envelopes, work orders, execution logs, proof/spec receipts, benchmark ratchets, policy-update leases, steward decisions, SCF transitions, release notes, and residual ledgers. The reference architecture would be judged by whether those records survive happy paths and blocked paths, not by how coherent the diagram looks. | `argument` |
| `project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference` | Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference | An implementation-reference crosswalk table from ASI stack layer to Theseus report, config, or tool surface, with evidence state and public claim boundary for each row. | Project Theseus becomes valuable here only as a public-safe implementation-reference lab. Project Theseus would remain useful to this book only if its reports, configs, gates, ledgers, work-board entries, residuals, and replay notes can be mined without turning private or local runs into general capability claims. A mature Theseus reference lane would provide importable report bundles rather than dashboard impressions. Each bundle would identify the ASI Stack layer it exercises, the command or report source, configuration, environment boundary, artifact checksum, gate result, residual, missing artifact, public claim boundary, and non-claims. A dashboard could visualize those bundles, but the report bundle would be the evidence object. | `argument` |
| `prototype-roadmap` | Prototype Roadmap | A phase table with deliverables, gates, blockers, and acceptance criteria. | The roadmap endpoint is a phase-gated build controller for the ASI Stack. The roadmap would not be a list of aspirations. It would be a dependency graph that decides which prototypes can be built, which are blocked, which are allowed only as research demos, and which may affect claim support after evidence review. A mature roadmap controller gives every phase explicit prerequisites, acceptance gates, commands, required artifacts, evidence refs, residuals, unlocks, phase debt, retirement criteria, and non-claims. A later phase could not borrow credibility from an earlier milestone unless the acceptance record exists and the downstream dependency matches the evidence boundary. | `argument` |
| `living-book-methodology` | Living Book Methodology | A public Quarto repo with dynamic scaffold, source matrix, claim matrix, proof manifest, validation, and GitHub Pages. | The endpoint is a research operating system that happens to render as a book. The live site would remain the canonical, AI-readable, researcher-auditable surface, while Human view, reader editions, ebook formats, and future audio scripts would be derived projections with explicit strip policies and release records. A mature living-book workflow would let a future agent ingest a new AI paper, mine its public-safe content, decide whether to update a chapter or add a precise new one, update the outline and manifest, generate proof/test backlog, draft or revise prose, validate source/evidence boundaries, render the live site, and prepare reader editions without hand-renumbering or hidden support-state changes. | `argument` |
| `open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan` | Open Research Agenda and Bibliography Plan | A split Corben-source-corpus and external-literature plan with source-note backlog and insertion/merge rules. | The bibliography endpoint is a source and research graph for the entire ASI Stack. The bibliography would not be an afterthought at the end of the book, and it would not blur Corben's papers, Corben-supplied materials, recovered project records, or local project sources with outside literature. It would be the routing table that tells future agents which ideas have sources, which sources have been read, which chapters they affect, which proof or experiment backlogs they create, which material is external, and which claims still cannot move. A mature source graph would give every paper, project, local note, external reference, conversation-mined packet, recovery item, and missing source a public-safety policy, ingestion state, source note, chapter assignments, deduplication status, evidence-transition preconditions, proof/test backlog, citation status, and non-claims. New material would update the research graph before it changes manuscript prose. | `argument` |
